name: Designing immersive spaces for future tech storytelling
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user, Ahab/Zayne Amason, is a production designer and fabricator based in LA with a Game Design degree from Full Sail University and about 1.5 years of fabrication experience. They operate a workshop with CNC router and laser cutting capabilities and are interested in positioning themselves at the intersection of physical fabrication, digital technology (AI, AR, VR), and experiential design. The user requested help recreating LinkedIn profile content options, specifically wanting to focus on being a designer for future tech in physical spaces that drives storytelling, interactions, creativity, and wonder, while explicitly avoiding emphasis on previous experience and not wanting to lean heavily into fabrication credentials.

Claude created multiple iterations of LinkedIn profile content based on the user's evolving preferences. Initially, Claude produced comprehensive profile options emphasizing fabrication capabilities and technical skills, but the user redirected toward design theory, future technology, and human interaction focus, wanting to appear intriguing rather than resume-focused. The user wanted people to be curious about them and want to have conversations, but not come across as just an "idea guy." After Claude created more philosophical, manifesto-style content, the user requested a significant tone shift toward being "professionally chill with a hint of confidence" - factual, truthful, and genuine rather than trying too hard.

The final approach focused on conversational, straightforward language that positioned the user's game design background as valuable experience design thinking that translates to physical spaces. Claude created multiple short, casual profile versions that mentioned fabrication capabilities as a grounding element rather than the main focus. The user selected "Version 5: Conversational" and requested Claude expand it into a 2000-token essay maintaining the same chill, genuine tone. The final essay covered the user's game design foundation, learning fabrication, the intersection of physical and digital design, their core interests in human experience, and their current positioning in LA, written as a thoughtful portfolio piece rather than promotional content.

chat_messages.text: I want to recreate these profile content option for me linked in profile using the articles and additional information i provided. Also attaching outline of my profile for context. I dont want to lean into military Im just just a year and a half into fabrication I want to really hone in on the idea of being a designer for future tech in a physical space that drives story telling , interactions , and creativity and wonder:

attachments.extracted_content:
EXPANDED PROFILE CONTENT OPTIONS
OPENING VARIATIONS
Story-Driven Openers:
A. "Six years ago, I walked into a fabrication shop for the first time. Today, I run one. Production designer specializing in bringing ambitious visions to life through hands-on fabrication and technical know-how. Every project starts with 'what if' and ends with 'we did it.'"
B. "Production designer with dirt under my fingernails and CAD files in the cloud. I bridge the gap between dreamers and builders, managing projects from first sketch to final install. Based in LA with a fully equipped workshop ready to tackle the impossible."
C. "I believe the best designs come from understanding how things are built. That's why I approach production design with both creative vision and fabrication expertise—ensuring what looks good on screen works even better in reality."
D. "From Army medic to production designer—different uniform, same mission-critical mindset. I create immersive environments and brand experiences that connect with audiences on a visceral level. Specialized in fast-turnaround projects where failure isn't an option."
E. "Production designer who learned design backwards—started in the shop, ended up in the studio. This foundation in fabrication means I design with buildability in mind, saving clients time, money, and headaches."
F. "I help brands create moments people can't stop talking about. Production designer with in-house fabrication capabilities, turning concepts into experiences that live beyond the Instagram post."
G. "Every space tells a story. I'm a production designer who crafts those narratives in three dimensions—from intimate brand activations to festival-scale installations. Full-service design through fabrication, because the best stories deserve to be built right."
MIDDLE SECTION VARIATIONS
Project & Process Focus:
A. "My sweet spot is the $10K-$50K project that needs to look like a million bucks. I've developed systems for maximizing impact within real-world constraints—knowing when to go custom and when to cleverly repurpose. Recent work includes festival stages that toured three states, brand pop-ups that generated 2M social impressions, and corporate installations that actually made people smile at trade shows."
B. "The workshop is where ideas become real. Running a 2,000 sq ft fabrication space with CNC and laser capabilities means I can prototype rapidly, iterate physically, and deliver solutions that work the first time. This hands-on approach has saved dozens of productions from last-minute disasters."
C. "Game design degree meets production design career—an unexpected combination that's proven invaluable. Understanding user experience, environmental storytelling, and interactive systems helps me create spaces that don't just look good but actively engage audiences."
D. "Projects I love: The ones everyone else thinks are impossible. The 72-hour turnarounds. The 'we need this to transform into something else' challenges. The 'budget is tight but vision is huge' conversations. Built a reputation on delivering these consistently."
E. "My process eliminates the expensive surprises. By handling both design and fabrication, I catch the problems in CAD, not on install day. This integrated approach typically saves 30% on project costs and cuts timeline by a week."
F. "Collaboration is everything. I work closely with lighting designers, projection artists, audio engineers, and event producers to ensure seamless integration. Your vision becomes our shared mission, executed with military precision and creative flexibility."
Skills & Capabilities Focus:
A. "Technical toolkit includes everything from traditional woodworking to parametric design. Fluent in the full Adobe suite, comfortable in 3D from Blender to Unreal, and experienced in fabrication from CNC to welding basics. But the real skill? Knowing which tool solves which problem."
B. "What I bring to the table: Design thinking that accounts for transport, setup, strike, and storage. Rendering skills that actually represent buildable objects. Project management that assumes Murphy's Law is a guarantee. And the hands-on experience to jump in when things get complicated."
C. "Fabrication capabilities: CNC routing up to 4'x8' sheets, laser cutting for detailed work, full woodshop for traditional construction. Digital pipeline: concept sketches → 3D modeling → technical drawings → CNC files → finished pieces. Everything under one roof."
CLOSING VARIATIONS
Action-Oriented Closings:
A. "Currently booking Q1/Q2 2025 projects. Especially interested in music festivals, experiential marketing, and anything that pushes creative boundaries. Let's discuss how to make your next event unforgettable."
B. "Open to production design contracts, technical consultation, and fabrication partnerships. If you have a vision that needs to exist in the real world, I'd love to help make it happen. Workshop tours available for serious inquiries."
C. "Seeking opportunities that value both creative excellence and technical execution. Whether you need a full production designer or fabrication problem-solver, I bring solutions that work in reality, not just in renderings."
D. "Ready for the next challenge. Particularly drawn to projects with purpose—brand experiences that matter, events that create community, installations that inspire. Available for both local LA projects and travel-ready for the right opportunity."
E. "Let's build something amazing together. From concept development to load-out, I'm here to ensure your vision becomes an experience people remember. First consultation is always free—bring your wildest ideas."
F. "Available for immediate start on urgent projects, or happy to plan ahead for 2025 productions. Special interest in sustainable design practices and reusable/modular systems. Your project deserves someone who cares about every detail."
G. "Looking to join a team that values innovation, craftsmanship, and getting it right. Whether that's as a production designer, technical director, or creative fabrication partner, I'm ready to contribute to something meaningful."
Philosophy/Values Closings:
A. "I believe great design should be accessible, buildable, and memorable. If you're looking for someone who treats your budget with respect, your timeline as sacred, and your vision as a collaborative journey, let's connect."
B. "Design is a conversation between imagination and physics. I'm here to facilitate that dialogue, ensuring your creative vision thrives within real-world parameters. Available for projects that push boundaries while respecting budgets."
C. "Every project teaches something new. After 30+ builds, I'm still excited by the challenge of making something that's never existed before. If you're looking for a partner who brings enthusiasm and expertise in equal measure, let's talk."

Mix and match these elements based on the specific opportunities you're targeting. The key is maintaining authenticity while showcasing your unique value proposition—you're not just another designer, you're the one who can actually build the vision.

Looking at your LinkedIn against the production designers you're tracking, here's the real breakdown:
Where You Stand
Experience Gap: You're at 6+ years vs. Ilan Wainstein's 17+ years - that's expected, but you're solidly mid-level, not junior. The others (Jason, Toshi) are closer to your range at 8-11 years.
Network Size: Your 249 connections is actually decent - right between Jason's 107 and Ilan's 302. Toshi's 500+ is an outlier.
Client Prestige Gap: This is your biggest difference. Ilan lists Netflix, Google, Microsoft, Meta. You've got The Opus Group and smaller contracts. That's the tier jump you're trying to make.
What's Working For You
Your hands-on fabrication skills are unique - none of these other designers mention CNC, laser cutting, or actual build experience
Military background + EMT credentials = reliability/crisis management that others don't have
Full production pipeline experience (design through installation)
What They're Doing Better
Portfolio Presentation: Ilan has dedicated portfolio site prominently linked. Yours mentions projects but no visual showcase link.


Headline Optimization: Ilan's is "Production Designer | Experiential & Set Designer" - descriptive and searchable. Yours is just "Production Designer"


Credential Stacking: Ilan's Master's from Central Saint Martins carries weight. Toshi has Cannes Lions awards listed.


Geographic Flexibility: Several list multiple work locations or "legally authorized to work in US/EU" - broadens opportunities.


Actionable Fixes
Add "Fabrication Specialist" or "Technical Production Designer" to your headline
Get a portfolio site up ASAP - even a simple Cargo Collective showing your $10-20K projects
List specific software/tools in your About section (they all do this)
Add quantifiable wins: "30+ projects delivered" "98% on-time completion" whatever's true
Your Game Design degree is actually valuable for experiential/interactive work - lean into that crossover
You're positioned between the pure graphic designers (Jason) and the high-end production designers (Ilan). Your fabrication skills are your differentiator - none of them can actually BUILD what they design.

I have a workshop, so I prototype and build. Keeps the work grounded.

If you're doing something interesting in experiential design, I'm always curious to hear about it."
